full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Robinson Meyer, a staff writer for The Atlantic,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> wrote a short article after meeting 16-year-old climate activist Greta Thunberg<|15.00|> <|15.00|> in the wake of her stirring testimonials at this past week's United Nations General Assembly.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.86|> She looks younger than her years,<|3.84|> <|3.84|> yet her speeches take a shaming, authoritative tone<|8.24|> <|8.24|> that is, at the very least, unusual for a child,<|12.42|> <|12.42|> Robinson Meyer writes.<|15.08|> <|15.08|> How dare you?<|16.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "You have stolen my dreams and my childhood," she told world leaders at the United Nations today.<|7.60|> <|7.60|> She has also said that money and eternal economic growth are fairy tales.<|14.60|> <|14.60|> So she has inspired both public adoration and maligned theorizing."<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.48|> The harshest, most public criticism of this teenager<|7.12|> <|7.12|> has come from very powerful public adult men.<|13.72|> <|14.92|> Curious.<|16.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> And there is no way to know why exactly she has gotten under their skin,<|6.54|> <|6.54|> and I will not practice amateur psychosocial analysis of them<|11.72|> <|11.72|> in this reflection that appears to be a waste of time.<|16.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Greta, like prophets before her, are so, in the words of Candace Simpson, a friend<|7.94|> <|7.94|> of mine, so attuned to the present that they appear to tell the future.<|17.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.92|> They are so attuned to the present that they appear to tell the future,<|4.96|> <|4.96|> and there is something so poetic about prophetic work<|9.44|> <|9.44|> that it can simultaneously disarm and enrage at the same time,<|16.16|> <|16.80|> not unlike a long-awaited diagnosis after mysterious illness.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.20|> And poetry, according to my friend Mark Oakley,<|5.08|> <|5.64|> puts language in a state of emergency.<|10.84|> <|11.98|> Poetry is not an easy running river.<|15.58|> <|15.58|> It is not a quick read.<|18.12|> <|18.12|> It is a fountain,<|20.48|> <|20.48|> a source from which meaning can be slowly, patiently drawn.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Poetry is language that listens.<|4.24|> <|4.24|> There is no quick clarity, no seductive, easy answers."<|12.32|> <|12.32|> That is the disarming message we hear in the voice of the prophet Amos,<|21.44|> <|21.44|> who despairs over the socioeconomic state of things in his beloved nation.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He pronounces doom on those resting comfortably in urban centers while those on the outskirts go without.<|8.64|> <|9.64|> And the disarming story we hear from Jesus about a man he names Lazarus and a wealthy man who remains unnamed,<|19.28|> <|20.10|> disarms and enrages us all the same.<|24.46|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The wealthy man sees Lazarus, laden with open sores, outside the gate of his home, everyday<|10.14|> <|10.14|> begging, and he does nothing to improve his material existence.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and Lazarus dies, and so does the rich man,<|5.70|> <|5.70|> and Lazarus ends up at the side of Abraham,<|9.50|> <|9.50|> or in the old translations, in the bosom of Abraham,<|13.24|> <|13.24|> and the rich man ends up in torment.<|17.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.70|> Yet according to Dr. Nancy Lynn Westfield,<|6.30|> <|7.90|> the rich man, quote, "still feels entitled to ask for service."<|15.90|> <|16.30|> Can you believe that?<|18.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.92|> He still feels entitled to ask for service, care, and consideration<|6.60|> <|6.60|> from those of the lower class.<|8.52|> <|8.52|> He is expecting Lazarus to do his bidding even in death,<|14.32|> <|14.32|> even from Hades.<|15.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> even from the place of the dead.<|1.66|> <|2.06|> Even in Hades, Dr. Westfield says,<|4.24|> <|4.66|> the rich man wants the labor of the poor to support his family.<|8.20|> <|8.62|> Even from Hades, the rich man believes<|10.72|> <|10.72|> he has more privilege than Lazarus, who is in heaven.<|14.10|> <|14.46|> The storyteller helps us see the distorted values of society,<|18.58|> <|18.98|> the oppressive systems that keep these values in place,<|22.42|> <|22.42|> and the ironic ways Christians portend faithfulness."<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.64|> Not even death and the torment of apathy<|8.64|> <|8.64|> can change the rich man's posture toward those socially beneath him.<|16.32|> <|16.32|> Not even death.<|18.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.04|> Which means if the rich man were watching television today,<|4.46|> <|4.46|> he'd most likely yell at the screen when Greta Thunberg says,<|8.96|> <|8.96|> "You have stolen my dreams and my childhood with your empty words."<|15.34|> <|16.80|> Because this time, this fragile earth, our island home, is Lazarus.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.10|> And no amount of artful, disarming words, poetry,<|6.04|> <|6.04|> can change the minds of those who prefer denial to survival.<|11.62|> <|14.54|> Fading coral reefs and rising sea levels<|19.42|> <|19.42|> and scorching communities of color in major American cities<|24.12|> <|24.12|> are the open source of our time.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.30|> not to mention a steady rise in white supremacist violence<|5.78|> <|5.78|> targeting black and brown people across this country.<|9.94|> <|11.74|> This earth,<|13.74|> <|15.08|> this planet,<|17.08|> <|17.48|> is the neglected body lying at the gate of the powerful.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> An embodied witness to what decades of aggressive commercialization and unrestrained capitalism can do in the face of collective inaction, apathy, and aggression.<|15.80|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.84|> And yet, Greta stares us in the face,<|7.40|> <|7.40|> Lazarus stares us in the face,<|9.74|> <|9.74|> the earth stares us in the face,<|13.82|> <|13.82|> saying, "What will you make of me,<|19.28|> <|20.58|> of my suffering, of my life?"<|25.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And we pray with everything in us for the will to feel and to act.<|13.04|> <|14.56|> Amen.<|15.24|>
